student_name: Eliane Portela Nogueira Lima de Brito
hub_subtitle: Business English for a doctor rebuilding her professional voice

lesson.menu_title: Fielding the Hard Questions — Handling Investor Q&A Under Pressure
lesson.menu_desc: The third conditional (If we had shown the risk, they would not have pushed back) to reflect on a tough investor Q&A and handle the next one with control -- 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 14 -- Fielding the Hard Questions
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Eliane Portela | Aula 14 -- Fielding the Hard Questions: Handling Investor Q&A Under Pressure

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: resistance or disagreement to your idea
to question someone hard and repeatedly
to turn a question away without answering it
to avoid giving a clear, committed answer
to admit that a point is fair or true
a sign that warns of a possible problem
the worst possible outcome you can imagine
to remove someone's doubt or worry
to expect something and prepare for it
a further question or action after the first one

paras: The pitch ends and the real test begins: the questions. Every founder eventually learns that investors do not write checks because the slides were beautiful; they write them because the founder held up under pressure. When a room starts to grill you, each question is a small stress test. Some are genuine; others are designed to see whether you deflect, whether you hedge, or whether you can concede a fair point without losing the room. Eliane left her last pitch replaying every answer. If she had anticipated the hardest question, she told herself, she would have answered it in one calm sentence instead of three nervous ones.

The most common mistake under scrutiny is to treat every objection as an attack. A sharp investor is not trying to humiliate you; a good objection is almost a gift, because it shows you exactly where the red flag is. Founders who reassure a room do not pretend the risk does not exist. They name the worst-case scenario before the investor does, and they explain the contingency. Eliane still remembers one meeting that fell apart over a single number. If the team had shown a worst-case scenario in the first place, she now believes, the investors would not have pushed back so hard, and the follow-up questions would have felt like curiosity instead of doubt.

Learning to field hard questions is mostly learning in hindsight. After every Q&A, the strongest founders run a quiet post-mortem: which answer landed, which one they hedged, where they should have conceded and where they were right to hold firm. If I had deflected less and reassured more, one founder wrote, they would have trusted the plan sooner. None of this means having a perfect answer for everything. It means anticipating the pushback, welcoming the objection, and knowing that the room is not really testing your slides. It is testing whether, under real pressure, you are someone an investor would have backed.
reading.source: Adapted from a founder's guide to investor Q&A, After the Pitch

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: The best strategy under hard questions is to deflect and hedge every objection until the investor gives up and stops asking.

The Q&A after a pitch is the real test: strong founders welcome each objection, name the worst-case scenario and the red flag before the investor does, reassure the room, and learn in hindsight what they would have done differently.
The text argues that beautiful slides are the main reason investors write checks and that the questions afterward do not really matter.

items: Investors write checks mainly because the slides look beautiful.
The text says they write checks not because the slides were beautiful, but because the founder held up under pressure.
A good objection is almost a gift, because it shows the founder where the red flag is.
The text says a sharp investor is not trying to humiliate you and that a good objection shows you exactly where the red flag is.
Strong founders wait for the investor to name the worst-case scenario first.
The text says founders who reassure a room name the worst-case scenario before the investor does.
After every Q&A, the strongest founders run a quiet post-mortem to learn in hindsight.
The text says they run a quiet post-mortem after every Q&A: which answer landed, which one they hedged, where they should have conceded.

gapfill.text: Notice how the words work together in a Q&A: you anticipate the pushback, you stand up to the scrutiny, you welcome each objection instead of treating it as an attack, you spot the red flag, you name the worst-case scenario, you reassure the room, and you never deflect or hedge a fair point -- you concede it and move to the follow-up. This is the language a founder uses to field hard questions with control.

bank.label: Your Q&A survival toolkit
items: If I had anticipated that question, I would have answered in one calm sentence.
A good objection is almost a gift; it shows me where the red flag is.
We name the worst-case scenario first, so the room does not push back.
If we had shown the contingency earlier, they would not have hedged.
Under scrutiny, do not deflect -- concede the fair point and move on.
If she had reassured the investors sooner, they would have trusted the plan.
Welcome the pushback; the follow-up questions are where deals are won.
Had we prepared for the grilling, we could have closed the round.

lf.title: Third conditional: spot the two halves
items: the question, she would have stayed calm.
They would have invested if the plan
If we had shown the risk, they
for the follow-up, we could have closed the round.
If I had deflected less, they

lf.text: A, B, C and E all follow one pattern: If + past perfect (had anticipated / had shown / had deflected) in the condition, and would / could / might + have + past participle in the result. This is the third conditional: an imagined past that did not actually happen. It is the grammar of hindsight and regret -- what would have been true if the past had been different. D shows the formal inversion: Had we prepared... means the same as If we had prepared... but drops the if.

items.situation: Think of a question that once caught you off guard. Say what you would have done differently, using the third conditional and the word 'anticipate'.
tips: If I had anticipated that question, I would have answered in one calm sentence.
If I had prepared for it, I would not have hedged the way I did.

items.situation: An investor finds a red flag you did not mention. React well, using 'concede' or 'reassure'.
tips: You are right to raise it -- let me concede that point and show you the contingency.
That is a fair objection. Let me reassure you: here is our worst-case scenario and our plan for it.

items.situation: Explain what would have happened if your team had shown the worst-case scenario earlier. Use the third conditional.
tips: If we had shown the worst-case scenario earlier, they would not have pushed back so hard.
Had we named the risk first, the follow-up questions would have felt like curiosity.

items.situation: Someone grills you on your numbers. Show that you welcome the scrutiny, using 'pushback' or 'objection'.
tips: I welcome the pushback; a sharp objection shows me exactly where to look.
Grill me on the numbers -- every objection makes the plan stronger.

items: Think of your last tough Q&A. In hindsight, say three things you would have done differently, using the third conditional (If + had + past participle, ... would have ...).
An investor names a risk you did not mention. Respond well: concede the fair point, name the worst-case scenario, and reassure the room with your contingency.
Role-play the moment the room starts to grill you. Show that you welcome the pushback: turn each objection into a follow-up that makes your case stronger -- without deflecting or hedging.
Run a one-minute post-mortem out loud: which answers landed, where you hedged, and what you would have changed. Then anticipate the questions you will face next time.

answerkey.title: Reveal the model language
list: The replay: 'If I had anticipated the question about year one, I would have named the worst-case scenario myself. If I had hedged less, they would have trusted the plan sooner.'
The red flag: 'You are right, and I concede the point. The worst-case scenario is a slow first year, so we built a contingency for it -- let me reassure you with the numbers.'
Welcome the grilling: 'Grill me on this. A sharp objection is a gift; it shows me the red flag. I would rather face the pushback now than discover it after we sign.'
The post-mortem: 'The value proposition landed. I hedged on the numbers. If we had shown the contingency in the deck, they would not have pushed back so hard. Next time I anticipate that follow-up.'
answerkey.note: Notice how each answer rides on the third conditional -- If + had + past participle, would have + past participle -- to turn a past mistake into a lesson, and how the pressure words (objection, red flag, pushback, reassure) frame the Q&A as a conversation, not an attack.

listenings.text: Hi Eliane, it is your pitch coach. One thought before your next Q and A. The room is not testing your slides, it is testing you under pressure. When they grill you, do not deflect and do not hedge. If you had watched last week's meeting, you would have seen it: the founders who win are the ones who concede a fair point and then reassure the room. Anticipate the hardest question, and name the worst-case scenario before the investor finds the red flag. Remember, a sharp objection is almost a gift. If you had welcomed the pushback last time instead of fearing it, every follow-up question would have become a chance to prove your plan. Field the hard ones well, and the easy ones take care of themselves.

Hi team, it is Eliane. Let me run a quick post-mortem on the Q and A. Two things I would have changed. First, if I had anticipated the question about year one, I would have named the worst-case scenario myself, instead of hedging. Second, if we had shown the contingency in the deck, they would not have pushed back so hard on the numbers. Here is what we do next time. We do not deflect a single objection; we treat each one as a red flag we get to fix in the room. We concede the fair points fast, we reassure with data, and we welcome every follow-up question. If we grill ourselves before the investors do, the real Q and A will feel like a conversation, not a test.
